# Git and GitHub

Git and GitHub are universal requirements for all emLab coding projects. *Git* is a version control system that tracks changes to your code on your local computer, maintaining a complete history of every modification. When you use Git, a complete history of all the changes made to your code are stored in a folder alongside your project files, known as a *repository* (or "repo"). *GitHub* is the online platform where we host these repositories and collaborate with others using tools for code review and project management. In short: Git is the tool you use locally to track changes, while GitHub is where you store and share those repositories with your team.

Why Git and GitHub?

- **Promotes transparency and reproducibility** by preserving a complete, time-stamped history of changes and making it easy to recover earlier versions.
- **Improves code quality** by protecting a working version as new features are developed and refined.
- **Enables collaboration** by letting multiple contributors plan their approach and work in parallel with Issues, branches, pull requests, and structured code review.
- **Speeds onboarding** because new team members can quickly understand project history and current priorities.
- **Supports open and reproducible science** by making methods, analysis code, and revision history accessible to collaborators, reviewers, future researchers, and the public.

**All emLab coding work must live in a repository under the [emlab-ucsb](https://github.com/emlab-ucsb) GitHub organization. The sections below cover everything you need to know, from initial setup through advanced workflows.**

- [Getting set up](01-setup.qmd) -- Joining the emLab GitHub organization, installing git, and authenticating with GitHub.
- [Repository organization](02-repo-organization.qmd) -- How to structure a repository and write an effective README.
- [Version control with commits](03-commits.qmd) -- What commits are and how to write a good commit message.
- [Using Issues](04-issues.qmd) -- How to use GitHub Issues to track work, document decisions, and communicate within a project.
- [Working with branches](05-branches.qmd) -- What branches are and how to use them in practice.
- [Pull Requests](06-pull-requests.qmd) -- How to open, review, and merge a pull request.
- [Rulesets](07-rulesets.qmd) -- Protecting the main branch and enabling automated review.
- [Effective workflows](08-workflows.qmd) -- What git-based development looks like in practice, for solo and collaborative work.
- [Public repositories for publications](09-public-repos.qmd) -- How to prepare a repository for public release alongside a publication.
- [GitHub Extensions in Positron and VS Code](10-gh-extensions.qmd) -- Using GitHub's integrations in Positron and VS Code to streamline your workflow.
- [GitHub Copilot](11-github-ai-assistants.qmd) -- An optional AI coding assistant available through GitHub.
